title: Blood on Cotton
stainChemistry: Blood contains hemoglobin, albumin, fibrin, and clotting proteins that bond to cotton cellulose through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. When fresh, these proteins are water-soluble and respond well to enzymatic treatment. Heat coagulates and cross-links the proteins permanently into the fiber — a heat-set blood stain on cotton is orders of magnitude harder to remove than a fresh one.
whyThisWorks: Protein Formula (digestant spotter) breaks down blood protein chains through enzymatic hydrolysis at neutral pH before coagulation occurs. NSD removes the loosened protein particles and digestant residue. A dilute acetic acid rinse restores the fabric to neutral pH. Cotton tolerates this full protocol without fiber risk.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried. Even dried blood responds to this protocol — rehydrate with cold water first before applying digestant.

steps: 1. Flush immediately with cold water from back of fabric
2. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) — tamp gently with spotting brush, work edge to center — dwell 5-10 minutes
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) — flush with cool water or steam gun
4. If residual discoloration on white/colorfast cotton: dilute hydrogen peroxide (3%) — dwell 5 min, flush
5. Neutralizing rinse: dilute acetic acid (1:10, 28% stock) — restores fabric pH
6. Blot dry — inspect before any heat or pressing
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — NEVER warm or hot
warnings: Never apply hot water — permanently coagulates blood proteins
Never use alkaline detergent without enzyme pre-treatment
Do not press or apply heat before stain is fully resolved
For colored cotton: test hydrogen peroxide on hidden seam before applying

steps: 1. Flush immediately with cold water from back of fabric — never front
2. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to stain — work in gently with fingers, cold water only
3. Let sit 10 minutes — do not apply heat
5. For white cotton: apply hydrogen peroxide (3%) to residual discoloration — dwell 5-10 min, cold rinse
6. For dried stain: soak in cold water 30 minutes first to rehydrate, then treat
7. Air dry and inspect — do not machine dry until stain is confirmed removed
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Take to a professional if stain has been through a dryer cycle, or if it remains after two cold treatment attempts.
warnings: No hot water at any point
No dryer before stain is removed
Hydrogen peroxide may lighten dyed cotton — test first

fiberSensitivities: Cotton: wide treatment latitude — tolerates enzymes, mild bleach, most pH ranges
Colored cotton: always test H2O2 before use
Cotton blends with silk or wool: follow silk/wool protocol, not cotton

escalation.when: Stain has been through a dryer, or remains after two cold enzyme treatments
escalation.whatToTell: Blood on cotton — may be heat-set. Ask for cold Protein Formula (digestant) pre-spotting before any pressing.
escalation.specialistType: Any competent dry cleaner or wet cleaner with enzyme spotting

deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh: excellent. Dried: cold rehydrate first. Heat-set: professional. H2O2 only if colorfast confirmed.
